teaching_arc.entry_point: Students know SOH-CAH-TOA from triangles, but may not yet connect circular motion, unit-circle coordinates, and trig graphs as one system.
teaching_arc.progression: Open with the Ferris wheel hook, define sin/cos on the unit circle, build ASTC and identities, then graph behavior, worked examples, a sign trap, and a cosine transformation pause before the full-width lab.
teaching_arc.destination: Students can evaluate any angle with reference angles, read transformed trig graphs, and explain why periodic functions matter before limits.
teaching_arc.tone: Clear, warm, visual, active, and personal-brand friendly.
methods: Use interactive unit-circle and graph widgets as the main explanation surface.
Use short worked steps for examples so students can follow without a wall of text.
Name the square-root sign mistake before it becomes a habit.
During narrated playback, move θ or graph parameters when the instructor names them.

audience_level: first-year university and advanced high-school students who completed Topic 1 (functions review) and need trig fluency before limits
topic_goal: Rebuild trigonometry around the unit circle so students can evaluate angles, read graphs, and carry periodic intuition into Session 2 limits.
topic_takeaways: Read sin θ and cos θ as y- and x-coordinates on the unit circle.
Use ASTC plus reference angles to evaluate any angle quickly.
Apply the Pythagorean identity with ± and quadrant logic.
Read amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift on sine and cosine graphs.
Connect circular motion (Ferris wheel) to periodic graphs students will see in limits and derivatives.
style_notes: Keep the tone personal, warm, and precise, as if teaching directly to a YouTube viewer.
Welcome students back to Arian University, do NOT re-introduce Arian from scratch; Topic 1 already did that.
On learning-objective slides, orient students on the course roadmap before listing skills.
Name Topic 1 as completed, this topic as current, and Session 2 Limits as next, especially the Squeeze Theorem needing sin x / x.
Move between circle, graph, formula, and plain-language interpretation on every example.
Use student-prediction moments before revealing ASTC signs, exact values, or transformation parameters.
When a widget is present, call out θ or A/B/C/D, then let the scripted timeline move while explaining.
For website mode, invite students to drag θ after the narrated pass.
On dense visual slides, keep the avatar low so the circle or graph carries focus.
For pause slides, give a real beat, then answer with one concrete number or graph feature.
transition_style: Use each slide as one teaching beat: name the task, point to the visual, move the interactive state, preview the next tool.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome back with a motion hook: limits soon need sin x / x and periodic bounds. Frame this as Topic 2 in Session 1 after the function toolkit, not a detour, but the language of cycles.
must_cover: Welcome back, Topic 2 in Session 1 Preliminaries, after Review of Functions and Graphs.
Trigonometry is the periodic toolkit limits and derivatives assume.
Session 2 is Limits and Continuity; the Squeeze Theorem will use sin x / x.
This session stays visual and interactive, unit circle first, graphs second.
must_say: If you rode a Ferris wheel, your height traces a sine wave, that is where we start.
By the end you will read any angle on the unit circle and sketch transformed trig graphs.
slides.transition_from_previous: Topic 1 built function language; this topic adds the periodic piece.
slides.transition_to_next: Show the roadmap and the five skills students will leave with.
likely_student_confusion: Students may treat trig review as optional. Tie it directly to limits and the Squeeze Theorem.
slides.explanation_style: Brief welcome-back, then immediate hook, no long re-introduction.
slides.story_hint: Start with motion students can picture: up-and-down on a wheel.
slides.delivery_goal: Students feel why trig matters for calculus within 15 seconds.
slides.scene_hint: Face camera for welcome-back, then gesture toward the upcoming Ferris wheel visual.

slides.narration_seed: Use the roadmap: Topic 1 completed, Topic 2 current, Session 2 Limits next. Keep objectives brisk, five bullets map to the five blocks of the lecture.
must_cover: Point to the course roadmap before reading objectives.
Session-level framing: five sessions total; Session 1 Preliminaries; Session 2 Limits next.
Do not say topic X of Y aloud, the grid shows progress.
Five skills: ASTC evaluation, exact values, identities, graph parameters, Ferris wheel model.
slides.transition_from_previous: The title named the session; this slide turns it into a promise.
slides.transition_to_next: Drop into the Ferris wheel metaphor, the hook students can feel.
likely_student_confusion: Too many skills at once, tell them the lecture builds one tool at a time.
slides.explanation_style: Roadmap first, then one-line per objective.
slides.story_hint: Frame objectives as a checklist they can tick during the video.
slides.delivery_goal: Students know where they are in Calculus 1 and what payoff to expect.

slides.narration_seed: Ride the metaphor briefly, then drive the ferris_link widget: drag θ from 0 to 2π and narrate height = sin θ, horizontal = cos θ staying locked to the same angle.
must_cover: Height above center behaves like sin θ; horizontal distance like cos θ.
Same rotation angle θ controls both, that linkage is the whole lecture.
Unit circle is the Ferris wheel of radius 1.
Move θ through π/2, π, 3π/2 to show zeros and extrema.
slides.transition_from_previous: Objectives were abstract; circular motion makes trig tangible.
slides.transition_to_next: Formalize: sine and cosine are coordinates on the unit circle.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think sin and cos are unrelated formulas. Keep saying same θ.
slides.explanation_style: Narrate the widget state change as θ moves.
slides.story_hint: Tell it through the red dot on the wheel and the wave dots tracking it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students feel sin/cos as two views of one rotation.
slides.scene_hint: Scripted timeline should run during narration at π/2 and π.

slides.narration_seed: State the theorem box definitions, then use projections variant: red vertical leg is sin θ, blue horizontal leg is cos θ. Move θ through π/6, π/4, π/3, π/2.
must_cover: cos θ = x-coordinate, sin θ = y-coordinate on the unit circle.
tan θ = sin θ / cos θ when cos θ ≠ 0.
Radius 1 embeds x² + y² = 1, preview of the Pythagorean identity.
This definition works for any angle, not only acute triangles.
slides.transition_from_previous: The Ferris wheel gave motion; now the coordinate definition.
slides.transition_to_next: Coordinates have signs, ASTC tells which functions are positive.
likely_student_confusion: Students may swap sin and cos. Repeat: sine is vertical (y), cosine is horizontal (x).
slides.explanation_style: Formula first, then point to dashed legs on the circle.
slides.story_hint: Read the coordinate pair aloud as θ moves.
slides.delivery_goal: Students can define all three core ratios from one point on the circle.
slides.scene_hint: Highlight projection lines when naming sin and cos.

slides.narration_seed: Teach ASTC as coordinate logic, not magic. Drag θ through all four quadrants on the astc widget; run the pause reveal on QIII tangent.
must_cover: QI all positive; QII sine only; QIII tangent only; QIV cosine only.
In QIII both coordinates negative so tan = sin/cos is positive.
ASTC decides signs after reference angles give magnitudes.
Pause check: tangent positive in QIII.
slides.transition_from_previous: Definitions are unsigned; quadrants supply signs.
slides.transition_to_next: Introduce reciprocal names and their undefined points.
likely_student_confusion: Memorizing ASTC without coordinates. Tie each letter to x and y signs.
slides.story_hint: Color regions on the widget match the mnemonic.
slides.delivery_goal: Students pick correct signs without hesitation.
slides.scene_hint: Scripted timeline visits all four quadrants in order.

slides.narration_seed: Flip fractions, csc, sec, cot. Use the table for undefined points: sin = 0, cos = 0, tan = 0. Briefly move θ near π/2 and 0 on the circle.
must_cover: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan.
Undefined when denominator hits zero.
sec blows up at odd multiples of π/2, same places cos = 0.
slides.transition_from_previous: ASTC handled signs; reciprocals rename the same numbers.
slides.transition_to_next: The Pythagorean identity links sin and cos algebraically.
likely_student_confusion: Fear of csc/sec/cot names, reassure they are just reciprocals.
slides.explanation_style: Table anchors, one sentence per reciprocal pair.
slides.story_hint: Denominator zero is the only story here.
slides.delivery_goal: Students flip sin/cos/tan to reciprocals without panic.

slides.narration_seed: Three-step derivation from x² + y² = 1. Emphasize this is the identity used to find cos from sin in the tricky example later. Move θ on the circle while citing the triangle legs.
must_cover: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 from the unit circle equation.
Proof: substitute (cos θ, sin θ) into x² + y² = 1.
Solving for cos θ from sin θ introduces ±, quadrant picks the sign.
This identity appears again on the tricky example slide.
slides.transition_from_previous: Reciprocals renamed ratios; Pythagorean links sin and cos.
likely_student_confusion: Treating the identity as only for right triangles. Stress all angles.
slides.explanation_style: Walk derivation steps one at a time.
slides.story_hint: Hypotenuse 1 on the circle makes the proof one line.
slides.delivery_goal: Students cite the identity and know ± comes next.

slides.narration_seed: sin and cos repeat every 2π, one full turn. tan repeats every π because both coordinates flip sign. Switch widget from sine to tangent; point at asymptotes.
must_cover: sin(θ + 2π) = sin θ, cos(θ + 2π) = cos θ.
tan(θ + π) = tan θ.
Reference angles work because of periodicity.
Tangent vertical asymptotes where cos θ = 0.
slides.transition_to_next: Even and odd symmetry, another graph shortcut.
likely_student_confusion: Forgetting tangent's period is π, not 2π.
slides.explanation_style: Graph switch at the moment you name tangent period π.
slides.story_hint: One lap around the circle closes sine; half a lap flips both signs for tan.
slides.delivery_goal: Students know the three period constants.
slides.scene_hint: Timeline switches family to tangent at the tangent sentence.

slides.narration_seed: Odd: sin and tan negate when θ → −θ. Even: cos unchanged. Run pause on cos(−π/3). Timeline: sine graph, cosine graph, reflected sine.
must_cover: sin(−θ) = −sin θ, odd.
cos(−θ) = cos θ, even.
tan(−θ) = −tan θ, odd.
Pause: cos(−π/3) = cos(π/3) = 1/2.
slides.transition_from_previous: Periodicity is horizontal repeat; even/odd is mirror symmetry.
slides.transition_to_next: Combine parameters on transformed sine graphs.
likely_student_confusion: Applying even rule to sine. Test with f(−θ) vs −f(θ).
slides.story_hint: Cosine mirrors across the y-axis; sine rotates through the origin.

slides.narration_seed: Teach y = A sin(Bx + C) + D with the same care as Topic 1 shifts. Walk timeline: A=2, B=2, phase left, then A negative. Stress phase = −C/B.
must_cover: Amplitude |A|, period 2π/|B|, phase shift −C/B, vertical shift D.
Positive C inside sin(Bx + C) shifts left, same trap as f(x + c).
Negative A reflects the wave; amplitude still |A|.
Same rules apply to cosine.
slides.transition_from_previous: Symmetry was about θ → −θ; now parameters stretch and shift graphs.
slides.transition_to_next: Exact values at π/3, warm-up before reference angles.
likely_student_confusion: Phase shift sign error, connect to Topic 1 horizontal shift lesson.
slides.explanation_style: One parameter change per beat on the timeline.
slides.story_hint: Gray parent sin(x) stays visible as anchor.
slides.delivery_goal: Students read all four parameters from a formula.
slides.scene_hint: Animate amplitude before period before phase before reflection.

slides.narration_seed: Work π/3 on the special_angle widget. Reveal π/4 values in the pause block. Encourage memorizing the ladder π/6, π/4, π/3, π/2.
must_cover: Pause reveal: π/4 gives √2/2, √2/2, 1.
30-60-90 triangle inside the circle.
slides.transition_from_previous: Graph parameters were global; now exact numbers at one angle.
slides.transition_to_next: Generalize with reference angles and ASTC at 5π/3.
likely_student_confusion: Mixing up π/3 and π/6 values. Use the widget triangle.
slides.explanation_style: Step through example_solution steps with widget confirmation.
slides.story_hint: Coordinates on screen should match spoken values.
slides.delivery_goal: Students recall π/3 and π/4 without a calculator.

slides.narration_seed: Template drill: quadrant → reference angle → ASTC → π/3 values. 5π/3 is QIV, reference π/3, cos + only.
must_cover: 5π/3 is in QIV.
Reference angle 2π − 5π/3 = π/3.
QIV: cos +, sin −, tan −.
Final values: sin = −√3/2, cos = 1/2, tan = −√3.
slides.transition_from_previous: π/3 was direct; now an angle beyond 90°.
slides.transition_to_next: Harder: given sin θ, find cos θ with the identity.
likely_student_confusion: Calling 5π/3 QIII. it is QIV (between 3π/2 and 2π).
slides.explanation_style: Four-step loop spoken aloud with widget showing ref arc.
slides.story_hint: Blue reference arc is the acute helper.
slides.delivery_goal: Students can run the four-step loop alone.

slide_ref.source_title: Tricky Example: Given sin θ and Quadrant

slides.narration_seed: sin θ = −2/7 in QIII. Square the Pythagorean identity, take ±, pick negative cos. Rationalize tan. Foreshadow the next slide's mistake.
must_cover: cos²θ = 45/49 so cos θ = ±3√5/7.
QIII → cos θ = −3√5/7.
tan θ = 2√5/15 after rationalizing.
Both coordinates negative in QIII but tan positive.
slides.transition_from_previous: Reference-angle example had nice coordinates; now messy numbers.
likely_student_confusion: Stopping at the positive root for cos θ.
slides.explanation_style: Algebra on left, quadrant picture on right.
slides.story_hint: ± step is where exams are won or lost.
slides.delivery_goal: Students complete the loop with messy arithmetic.

slides.narration_seed: misconception_compare block: wrong = positive root only; right = ± then ASTC. Point to negative x-coordinate on the circle in QIII.
must_cover: √(x²) = |x|, not x.
Square root in algebra gives nonnegative magnitude; quadrant gives sign.
Wrong: cos θ = +3√5/7 in QIII.
Right: cos θ = −3√5/7 in QIII.
slides.transition_from_previous: The tricky example showed the correct path; this slide shows the trap.
slides.transition_to_next: Optional proof why tangent has period π.
likely_student_confusion: Calculator gives positive root, student must override with ASTC.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast wrong vs right side by side.
slides.story_hint: Point at the horizontal projection left of origin.
slides.delivery_goal: Students never auto-pick the positive cos θ.

slide_ref.source_title: [Challenge – Optional] Why Tangent Has Period π

slides.narration_seed: Optional: θ + π is antipodal on the circle, negating both coordinates. Ratio unchanged. Quick proof sketch then tangent graph shift.
must_cover: sin(θ + π) = −sin θ, cos(θ + π) = −cos θ.
tan(θ + π) = tan θ.
Explains shorter period and asymptote spacing.
Skip if time-limited, core course does not require the proof.
slides.transition_from_previous: Sign trap was algebraic; this is geometric depth.
slides.transition_to_next: Student pause on cosine transformation parameters.
likely_student_confusion: Thinking π shift works for sine the same way as tangent.
slides.story_hint: Opposite point on the circle is the whole story.
slides.delivery_goal: Interested students understand tan period π geometrically.

slide_ref.source_title: Pause and Try: Predict the Phase Shift

slides.narration_seed: YouTube pause beat. y = 2 cos(3x + π) − 1. Let students compute, then reveal: A=2, period 2π/3, phase −π/3 left, D=−1. Animate parent to full transform.
must_cover: Amplitude 2, period 2π/3.
Phase shift −π/3 (left), not +π/3.
Vertical shift −1.
Cosine family on the widget, same rules as sine.
must_say: Pause here and try all four parameters before I reveal.
slides.transition_from_previous: Challenge was optional theory; this is core skill check.
slides.transition_to_next: Full-width Ferris lab to play with the whole story.
likely_student_confusion: Phase shift sign, +π inside means shift left by π/3.
slides.explanation_style: Silence for thinking, then reveal block read clearly.
slides.story_hint: Connect C = π to Topic 1 horizontal shift trap.
slides.delivery_goal: Students self-check transformation reading on cosine.

slide_ref.source_title: Interactive Lab: Ferris Wheel ↔ Trig Graphs

slides.narration_seed: Full-width visual_lab. Let students drag θ, same ferris_link from the opening, now they understand every readout. Mention radius in real wheels scales amplitude.
must_cover: Circle, waves, and bars stay synchronized.
Height zero at θ = 0, π, 2π; max/min at π/2, 3π/2.
Real Ferris wheel radius scales amplitude, not the angle logic.
This is the periodic model behind physics and engineering.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause slide was pencil-and-paper; lab is full intuition.
likely_student_confusion: Treating lab as separate from lecture, tie back to slide 1.
slides.explanation_style: Shorter narration, let the widget lead.
slides.story_hint: Bookend: opened on Ferris wheel, close the loop here.
slides.delivery_goal: Students feel the whole topic as one linked picture.
slides.scene_hint: Avatar small; widget dominates the frame.

slides.narration_seed: Six bullets, map each to a moment in the video. Teaser Session 2: Squeeze Theorem, sin x / x, derivatives of sin and cos. End warmly; Topic 3 is limits.
must_cover: Unit circle coordinates define sin and cos.
ASTC + reference angles evaluate any angle.
Pythagorean identity with ± and quadrant.
Graph parameters A, B, C, D.
Period and even/odd symmetry.
Next: Session 2 limits, Squeeze Theorem needs today's trig.
slides.transition_from_previous: Lab was play; summary is the checklist to keep.
slides.transition_to_next: Topic 3: The Intuitive Concept of a Limit.
likely_student_confusion: Leaving with memorized formulas but no loop. Restate the four-step evaluation loop once.
slides.explanation_style: One takeaway per breath; point backward to examples.
slides.story_hint: Close the Session 1 arc: functions then trig, then limits.
slides.delivery_goal: Students leave with a toolbox and know what Session 2 assumes.
